title: Galbi Korean BBQ Short Ribs
description: Galbi, or Korean BBQ Short Ribs, offers an exquisite symphony of sweet, savory, and umami flavors, creating a truly unforgettable dining experience. The tender, juicy ribs are marinated in a blend of soy sauce, garlic, and Asian pear, then grilled to caramelized perfection. This beloved dish is a cornerstone of Korean cuisine, cherished for its rich taste and interactive grilling experience.

instructions.title: Prepare the Short Ribs
instructions.text: Place the flanken-cut short ribs in a large bowl and cover with cold water. Let them soak for 30-60 minutes, changing the water once or twice, to draw out any residual blood. This step helps ensure a clean flavor. After soaking, thoroughly pat the ribs dry with paper towels. Trim any excessive fat from the edges of the ribs, but leave some for flavor.

instructions.title: Prepare the Marinade Base
instructions.text: In a blender or food processor, combine the peeled and cored Asian pear, peeled yellow onion, garlic cloves, peeled ginger, and the white parts of one green onion stalk. Add 1/4 cup of water to aid blending. Process until the mixture is completely smooth and liquefied.

instructions.title: Combine the Marinade
instructions.text: Transfer the blended pear mixture to a large mixing bowl. Add the soy sauce, brown sugar, toasted sesame oil, mirin, and black pepper. Whisk all ingredients together until the sugar is fully dissolved and the marinade is well combined. Taste and adjust seasoning if necessary, adding a pinch more sugar or soy sauce to balance the sweet and savory notes.

instructions.title: Marinate the Short Ribs
instructions.text: Place the prepared short ribs into the bowl with the marinade, ensuring each piece is thoroughly coated. You can also use a large resealable bag. Cover the bowl or seal the bag and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or preferably 24 hours, to allow the flavors to deeply penetrate the meat and the Asian pear enzymes to tenderize the ribs.

instructions.title: Prepare for Grilling
instructions.text: About 30 minutes before grilling, remove the marinated ribs from the refrigerator to allow them to come closer to room temperature. This promotes more even cooking. Lightly scrape off any excess solids from the ribs to prevent them from burning on the grill, but keep a good amount of the marinade coating. Preheat your grill (charcoal or gas) to medium-high heat. If using an indoor grill pan, preheat it thoroughly.

instructions.title: Grill the Galbi
instructions.text: Place the short ribs on the hot grill. Grill for 3-5 minutes per side, turning occasionally, until they are beautifully caramelized, slightly charred, and cooked to your desired doneness. The thinness of the flanken cut means they cook relatively quickly. Avoid overcooking to maintain tenderness. For an extra layer of flavor, you can lightly brush the ribs with a small amount of fresh, unused marinade during the last few minutes of grilling.

instructions.title: Rest and Serve
instructions.text: Once cooked, remove the Galbi from the grill and let them rest for a few minutes before serving. This allows the juices to redistribute, ensuring maximum tenderness. Garnish with the remaining chopped green onions (green parts) and toasted sesame seeds. Serve immediately with freshly steamed rice, crisp lettuce leaves for ssam (wraps), ssamjang (spicy dipping sauce), and an array of traditional Korean banchan (side dishes).

tips: For the most tender ribs, marinate for a full 24 hours. The enzymes in the Asian pear are crucial for breaking down the meat fibers.
Do not overcrowd the grill. Cook in batches if necessary to maintain consistent grill temperature and achieve a good sear.
Serve Galbi with a variety of Korean side dishes (banchan) and fresh lettuce leaves to make ssam (wraps) for an authentic Korean BBQ experience.
article:
## History of Galbi Korean BBQ Short Ribs

Galbi, meaning "rib" in Korean, is one of the most iconic and beloved dishes in Korean cuisine, with a history deeply rooted in Korean culinary traditions. While various forms of grilled meat have existed for centuries, the specific preparation of marinated and grilled beef short ribs, as we know it today, gained prominence during the Joseon Dynasty. Initially a dish reserved for royalty and the wealthy due to the cost of beef, it slowly became more accessible over time. The flanken-cut (L.A. Galbi) style, where the ribs are cut across the bone into thin strips, gained popularity in the mid-20th century, particularly among Korean immigrants in Los Angeles, making it easier to eat and grill quickly. Galbi is not just food; it's a centerpiece for celebrations and family gatherings, symbolizing generosity and shared joy.

## Why This Recipe Works

This recipe for Galbi achieves its authentic, mouthwatering flavor through a precise balance of ingredients and techniques. The star is the marinade, which combines the natural sweetness and tenderizing power of Asian pear with savory soy sauce, aromatic garlic and ginger, and a touch of brown sugar for deep caramelization. The Asian pear is not just for sweetness; its natural enzymes (like bromelain in pineapple) effectively break down the tough fibers in the short ribs, ensuring a melt-in-your-mouth texture without artificial tenderizers. The flanken cut allows for quick cooking and maximum surface area for the marinade to penetrate, resulting in intensely flavorful and juicy ribs that are perfectly charred on the outside and tender within.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

Achieving perfect Galbi requires attention to a few key details. Firstly, always soak your short ribs in cold water to remove impurities; this step is vital for a clean, pure beef flavor. When preparing the marinade, ensure the Asian pear, onion, garlic, and ginger are thoroughly blended into a smooth liquid; chunky marinade can burn easily on the grill. Marination time is critical – while 4 hours is the minimum, 24 hours allows for optimal tenderizing and flavor infusion. When grilling, resist the urge to constantly flip the ribs; let them develop a beautiful, deep caramelization on each side before turning. High heat is essential for a good sear, but be mindful not to burn the sugars in the marinade, adjusting heat as needed. Finally, always let the cooked ribs rest for a few minutes before cutting and serving to retain their precious juices.

## Serving Suggestions

Galbi is best enjoyed as part of a traditional Korean BBQ spread, which emphasizes a communal and interactive dining experience. The most classic way to eat Galbi is to wrap a piece of the grilled meat in a fresh, crisp lettuce leaf (like red leaf, green leaf, or perilla leaves) along with a dab of ssamjang (a savory, spicy dipping sauce) and perhaps a slice of raw garlic or jalapeño. This creates a balanced bite of fresh, savory, and spicy flavors. Accompany your Galbi with a bowl of warm, fluffy steamed rice and a variety of banchan (small side dishes) such as kimchi, seasoned bean sprouts (kongnamul muchim), pickled radish, and spinach namul. A side of doenjang jjigae (fermented soybean paste stew) or sundubu jjigae (spicy soft tofu stew) also complements the rich flavors of the BBQ beautifully.

jsonLd.keywords: Korean, Korean, authentic, traditional, dinner, Korean, Galbi, BBQ, Short Ribs, Dinner, Authentic, Traditional, Beef
recipeIngredient: 2.5 lbs Beef Short Ribs (Flanken-cut) (L.A. Galbi style, about 1/2-inch thick)
1/2 cup Soy Sauce (Ganjang) (Korean or regular soy sauce)
2 tbsp Mirin (Rice Wine) (Or Cheongju (Korean rice wine))
2 stalks Green Onions (Scallions) (Chopped, for garnish and marinade)

recipeInstructions.name: Prepare the Short Ribs
recipeInstructions.text: Place the flanken-cut short ribs in a large bowl and cover with cold water. Let them soak for 30-60 minutes, changing the water once or twice, to draw out any residual blood.
